articles.title: Production Halt on Tomb Raider Series Due to Sophie Turner's Injury
articles.summary: Production on the upcoming Tomb Raider TV series has been temporarily halted after actress Sophie Turner sustained a minor injury. Turner, known for her role as Sansa Stark in Game of Thrones, is portraying Lara Croft, the iconic video game archaeologist, in this adaptation for Prime Video. A spokesperson confirmed the injury occurred recently, though it's unclear if it happened on or off set, and stated that filming has paused as a precaution to allow Turner time to recover. The duration of the pause and specific details of the injury were not disclosed. Turner had previously mentioned a back issue during training for the role, highlighting the physical demands of the part. The series features a notable cast including Sigourney Weaver and is written by Phoebe Waller-Bridge, based on the Tomb Raider game series that began in 1996. Lara Croft gained fame as a pioneering female character in gaming, and the show aims to continue her legacy with a rebooted, more grounded approach from 2013.

articles.title: Bronze Age Shield Returns to Scotland After Centuries
articles.summary: A Bronze Age shield, discovered in a peat bog in Beith, North Ayrshire around 1779, has been returned to Scotland for the first time in over 230 years. It was presented to the Society of Antiquaries of London in 1791 and remained there until now. The shield is on loan to the National Museum of Scotland for the "Scotland's First Warriors" exhibition opening this summer. Measuring 60-70cm in diameter, it features hammered bosses and ribs, with damage from spears and other weapons indicating it was used in combat. Historical records suggest it was buried upright in peat with six other shields, though their locations are unknown. Dr Matthew Knight, a senior curator, notes that the shield's intricate design and rarity suggest it belonged to a high-status individual. The burial of multiple shields might have been part of a ceremonial act, possibly symbolizing peace among communities. The exhibition will include other shields from the Borders and Aberdeenshire, allowing curators to compare them for similarities and differences.

articles.title: Oil Surges and Asian Markets Drop Amid Ongoing Iran Conflict
articles.summary: Global oil prices surged and Asian stock markets declined sharply on Monday as the US-Israel war with Iran entered its fifth week. Brent crude oil rose over 3% to above $115 a barrel, while US oil climbed to $101.62, marking potential record monthly gains. Japan's Nikkei 225 fell 2.8%, and South Korea's Kospi dropped nearly 3%. The escalation followed Iran-backed Houthi rebels in Yemen attacking Israel over the weekend, with Iran threatening further retaliatory strikes against US and Israeli targets. US President Donald Trump suggested in an interview that he could seize Iranian oil, including Kharg Island, comparing it to US actions in Venezuela. Additionally, Iran's parliament speaker warned of readiness against US troops as 3,500 more arrived in the Middle East. The conflict has heightened volatility in energy markets, with Iran threatening to attack ships in the Strait of Hormuz. Shipping expert Lars Jensen noted that even if the strait reopened, oil prices would continue rising due to delayed shipments, and he warned of broader impacts, including escalating food prices from disrupted fertilizer exports, potentially exceeding the 1970s oil crisis in severity.

articles.title: Sinner's Historic Sunshine Double and Rivalry with Alcaraz
articles.summary: Jannik Sinner achieved a historic feat by winning the 'Sunshine Double', capturing both the Indian Wells and Miami Open titles without dropping a single set. This makes him the eighth man and first since Roger Federer in 2017 to accomplish this. In the Miami final, he defeated Jiri Lehecka 6-4 6-4 in a rain-interrupted match, showcasing his dominance. Sinner's victory narrows the gap with world number one Carlos Alcaraz, who was eliminated early in Miami, reducing the points difference to 1,190. Sinner has now won 34 consecutive sets at Masters events, joining legends Novak Djokovic and Rafael Nadal in winning three straight titles at this level. His success is attributed to an improved serve, with 70 aces in Miami and only one service game lost, highlighting his precision. The rivalry between Sinner and Alcaraz intensifies as they push each other to higher levels, while other players struggle to keep up. Sinner reflected on his progress, emphasizing hard work and practice leading to these results. As the season moves to clay courts, their competition is expected to continue for top honors.

articles.title: Paapa Essiedu's Drama Babies Aims to Normalize Conversations on Pregnancy Loss
articles.summary: British actor Paapa Essiedu stars in the TV drama Babies, which focuses on the emotional challenges of pregnancy loss through the story of a couple, Lisa and Steven. Essiedu emphasizes that the show's success would be measured by its ability to help even one person discuss this often-silenced topic. To prepare, he immersed himself in research, consulting with specialists and midwives, as he lacked personal experience with parenthood. Co-star Siobhán Cullen also relied on medical guidance and personal insights from women who had undergone similar losses, highlighting the show's commitment to authenticity. The series addresses the underrepresentation of miscarriage in media, with Essiedu attributing the silence to societal discomfort around grief, which can foster stigma and isolation. Writer and director Stefan Golaszewski, known for award-winning comedies, drew from his own experiences to create a humble and honest narrative, though it is not autobiographical. Overall, Babies seeks to provide a platform for sharing and reducing the shame associated with pregnancy loss.

articles.title: The Shift in Tech Job Cut Explanations: From Efficiency to AI
articles.summary: In recent years, major technology companies such as Google, Amazon, and Meta have implemented widespread job reductions, but their justifications have evolved from terms like efficiency and over-hiring to focusing on artificial intelligence (AI). Executives now attribute workforce cuts to AI advancements, claiming that these technologies enable firms to accomplish more with fewer employees. For instance, Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg predicts that AI will significantly alter work dynamics by 2026, and his company has already eliminated hundreds of positions while increasing AI investment. Similarly, Jack Dorsey of Block announced plans to reduce nearly half of his workforce, emphasizing that AI tools allow smaller teams to perform better. However, critics express skepticism, noting that Dorsey has overseen multiple job cuts in the past without citing AI and suggesting that companies may use AI as a more palatable excuse to mask cost-cutting motives or shareholder pressures. Tech investor Terrence Rohan argues that blaming AI sounds more favorable than admitting to financial constraints, potentially improving public perception. This trend highlights a strategic shift in corporate messaging amid ongoing layoffs in the tech industry.
